[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Rocksteady%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Rocksteady%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Rocksteady%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Rocksteady%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(May 2010)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

is a [music genre](/wiki/Music_genre "Music genre") that originated in [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") around 1966.[[1]](#cite_note-BBC-1) A successor of [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and a precursor to [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"), rocksteady was the dominant style of music in Jamaica for nearly two years, performed by many of the artists who helped establish reggae, including harmony groups such as [the Techniques](/wiki/The_Techniques "The Techniques"), [the Paragons](/wiki/The_Paragons "The Paragons"), [the Heptones](/wiki/The_Heptones "The Heptones") and [the Gaylads](/wiki/The_Gaylads "The Gaylads"); soulful singers such as [Alton Ellis](/wiki/Alton_Ellis "Alton Ellis"),[[2]](#cite_note-zonareggae-2) [Delroy Wilson](/wiki/Delroy_Wilson "Delroy Wilson"), Bob Andy, [Ken Boothe](/wiki/Ken_Boothe "Ken Boothe") and [Phyllis Dillon](/wiki/Phyllis_Dillon "Phyllis Dillon"); musicians such as [Jackie Mittoo](/wiki/Jackie_Mittoo "Jackie Mittoo"), [Lynn Taitt](/wiki/Lynn_Taitt "Lynn Taitt") and [Tommy McCook](/wiki/Tommy_McCook "Tommy McCook").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The term *rocksteady* comes from a popular (slower) dance style which is the subject in the Alton Ellis song "Rocksteady" and matched the new sound. Some rocksteady songs became hits outside Jamaica, as with ska, helping to secure the international base reggae music has today.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Music genre that originated in Jamaica around 1966

This article is about the music genre. For the video game developer, see [Rocksteady Studios](/wiki/Rocksteady_Studios "Rocksteady Studios"). For other uses, see [Rocksteady (disambiguation)](/wiki/Rocksteady_(disambiguation) "Rocksteady (disambiguation)").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Rocksteady "Special:EditPage/Rocksteady") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this article. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Rocksteady"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Rocksteady%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Rocksteady%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Rocksteady%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Rocksteady%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Rocksteady%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Rocksteady%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(May 2010)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

| Rocksteady | |
| --- | --- |
| Stylistic origins | * [Ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") * [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") |
| Cultural origins | Mid-1960s, Jamaica |

**Rocksteady** is a [music genre](/wiki/Music_genre "Music genre") that originated in [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") around 1966.[[1]](#cite_note-BBC-1) A successor of [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and a precursor to [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"), rocksteady was the dominant style of music in Jamaica for nearly two years, performed by many of the artists who helped establish reggae, including harmony groups such as [the Techniques](/wiki/The_Techniques "The Techniques"), [the Paragons](/wiki/The_Paragons "The Paragons"), [the Heptones](/wiki/The_Heptones "The Heptones") and [the Gaylads](/wiki/The_Gaylads "The Gaylads"); soulful singers such as [Alton Ellis](/wiki/Alton_Ellis "Alton Ellis"),[[2]](#cite_note-zonareggae-2) [Delroy Wilson](/wiki/Delroy_Wilson "Delroy Wilson"), Bob Andy, [Ken Boothe](/wiki/Ken_Boothe "Ken Boothe") and [Phyllis Dillon](/wiki/Phyllis_Dillon "Phyllis Dillon"); musicians such as [Jackie Mittoo](/wiki/Jackie_Mittoo "Jackie Mittoo"), [Lynn Taitt](/wiki/Lynn_Taitt "Lynn Taitt") and [Tommy McCook](/wiki/Tommy_McCook "Tommy McCook").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The term *rocksteady* comes from a popular (slower) dance style which is the subject in the Alton Ellis song "Rocksteady" and matched the new sound. Some rocksteady songs became hits outside Jamaica, as with ska, helping to secure the international base reggae music has today.

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rocksteady&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Ska-Rock_Steady_on_D_and_Em64.png/500px-Ska-Rock_Steady_on_D_and_Em64.png)](/wiki/File:Ska-Rock_Steady_on_D_and_Em64.png)

Ska/rocksteady rhythm[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Play](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/7/76/Ska-Rock_Steady_on_D_and_Em64.mid/Ska-Rock_Steady_on_D_and_Em64.mid.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Ska-Rock_Steady_on_D_and_Em64.mid "File:Ska-Rock Steady on D and Em64.mid")

The Jamaican musicians and producers who developed rocksteady had grown up learning and playing jazz and had played through ska. In a similar way to what happened at Motown, the musicians responsible for playing this new sound would go jam in a jazz club after work.

Other influences were, most notably, American [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") – Fats Domino, Louis Jordan and many others –, [mento](/wiki/Mento "Mento"), [Calypso music](/wiki/Calypso_music "Calypso music") and African drumming, as well. By the time rocksteady developed American [soul music](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") was popular, and that had an influence as well.

The tempo became slower with the development of rocksteady than it had been in ska.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The guitar and piano players began to experiment with occasional accents around the basic offbeat pattern.

The slowing that occurred with rocksteady allowed bass players to explore more fat, dark, loose, slow tones than ska bass.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The slower tempo and smaller band-sizes in turn led to a much larger focus on the bass line in general, which eventually became one of the recognizable characteristics of Jamaican music. In rocksteady, the lead guitar often doubles the bass line, in the muted picking style created by [Lynn Taitt](/wiki/Lynn_Taitt "Lynn Taitt") (as on "Run for Cover" by [Lee "Scratch" Perry](/wiki/Lee_%22Scratch%22_Perry "Lee \"Scratch\" Perry")).[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Lyrics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rocksteady&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Lyrics")]

Due in part to the heavy borrowing from US soul songs, many rocksteady songs are love songs; e.g. "Sharing You" by [Prince Buster](/wiki/Prince_Buster "Prince Buster"), which is a cover of a soul singer [Mitty Collier](/wiki/Mitty_Collier "Mitty Collier")'s original, and "Queen Majesty" by [the Techniques](/wiki/The_Techniques "The Techniques"), which is a cover of "Minstrel and Queen" by [the Impressions](/wiki/The_Impressions "The Impressions").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

There are rocksteady songs about religion and the [Rastafari movement](/wiki/Rastafari_movement "Rastafari movement"), though not to the same extent as in reggae. Rocksteady coincided with the rise of [rude boys](/wiki/Rude_boy "Rude boy") and some rocksteady songs reflect this (usually negatively) such as "Rude Boy Gone A Jail" by [the Clarendonians](/wiki/The_Clarendonians "The Clarendonians") and, most famously, "Judge Dread" by Prince Buster.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Alton Ellis was anti-rudie,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] and Alton Ellis and the Flames' "Cry Tough", released before the term rocksteady was in vogue,[[10]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-10) urged Jamaicans in the ghettos to stay tough through the hard times.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rocksteady&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

As a popular musical style, rocksteady was short-lived - the genre's heyday only lasted about two years, from around summer 1966 until spring 1968. However, its influence can still be heard in rhythms used today.

Also, in the middle to later part of the decade, as ska began to fade in popularity and the optimism that accompanied Independence in 1962 dwindled, young people from the Jamaican countryside were flooding into the urban [ghettos](/wiki/Ghetto "Ghetto") of [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica")—in neighborhoods such as Riverton City, Greenwich Town and [Trenchtown](/wiki/Trenchtown "Trenchtown"). Many of them became delinquents who exuded a certain coolness and style. These unruly youths became known as [rude boys](/wiki/Rude_boy "Rude boy").

[Alton Ellis](/wiki/Alton_Ellis "Alton Ellis") is sometimes said to be the father of rocksteady for his hit "Rocksteady";[[2]](#cite_note-zonareggae-2) however, other candidates for the first rocksteady single include "Take It Easy" by [Hopeton Lewis](/wiki/Hopeton_Lewis "Hopeton Lewis"), "Tougher Than Tough" by [Derrick Morgan](/wiki/Derrick_Morgan "Derrick Morgan") and "Hold Them" by [Roy Shirley](/wiki/Roy_Shirley "Roy Shirley").

One account of rocksteady's inception comes from the film "Studio One Drummie and the history of rocksteady music": rock steady music started at Studio One in 1966 because when Joe Isaacs at age 15 replaced Skatalites drummer Lloyd Knibbs, Isaacs could not play drums fast enough to keep up with the pace of ska - music director Jackie Mittoo (The Mozart of Jamaica), slowed down the tempo.

Another account comes from a Jamaican radio interview, pianist [Gladstone Anderson](/wiki/Gladstone_Anderson "Gladstone Anderson") said that bandleader [Lynn Taitt](/wiki/Lynn_Taitt "Lynn Taitt") made a suggestion to slow the music down whilst recording "Take It Easy".[[12]](#cite_note-12) Taitt backed this up in a 2002 interview, stating: "I told 'Gladdy to slow the tempo and that's how Take It Easy and rocksteady came about. Rocksteady is really slow ska."[[13]](#cite_note-Campbell2012-13)

Rocksteady's dominance meant that all record labels of the time released music in the genre; Studio One, Treasure Isle, Bunny Lee and Prince Buster were predominant.

The [record producer](/wiki/Record_producer "Record producer") [Duke Reid](/wiki/Duke_Reid "Duke Reid") released Alton Ellis' "Girl I've Got a Date" on his Treasure Isle label, as well as recordings by [the Techniques](/wiki/The_Techniques "The Techniques"), [the Silvertones](/wiki/The_Silvertones "The Silvertones"), [the Jamaicans](/wiki/The_Jamaicans "The Jamaicans") and [the Paragons](/wiki/The_Paragons "The Paragons"). Reid's work with these groups helped establish the vocal sound of rocksteady.[[10]](#cite_note-LarkinGE-10) Some would consider the rocksteady years to be Treasure Isle's best.

Notable solo artists include [Delroy Wilson](/wiki/Delroy_Wilson "Delroy Wilson"), [Ken Boothe](/wiki/Ken_Boothe "Ken Boothe") and [Phyllis Dillon](/wiki/Phyllis_Dillon "Phyllis Dillon") (known as the "Queen of Rocksteady"). Other musicians who were crucial in creating rocksteady included keyboard player [Jackie Mittoo](/wiki/Jackie_Mittoo "Jackie Mittoo"), drummers Joe Isaacs and Winston Grennan, bassist Jackie Jackson and saxophonist [Tommy McCook](/wiki/Tommy_McCook "Tommy McCook"). When ska band [the Skatalites](/wiki/The_Skatalites "The Skatalites") disbanded (64/65—accounts vary) McCook went to work at the Treasure Isle label and Jackie Mittoo went to the Studio One label—these two artists/arrangers became instrumental in the way these two labels became dominant and helped to form the sound of Rocksteady.

Despite its short lifespan, rocksteady's influence is great. Many reggae artists began in rocksteady (and/or ska)—most commonly reggae singers grew out of rocksteady groups, e.g., Junior Byles came from the Versatiles, John Holt was in the Paragons, both Pat Kelly and Slim Smith sang with the Techniques (Pat Kelly sings lead on "You Don't Care") and Ronnie Davis was in the Tennors while [Winston Jarrett](/wiki/Winston_Jarrett "Winston Jarrett") was in the Righteous Flames. The Wailing Wailers were similarly a vocal harmony trio (modelled on the Impressions) who came from ska, through rocksteady and became a reggae band with just the one main vocalist.

[Derrick Harriott](/wiki/Derrick_Harriott "Derrick Harriott") noted, "Ask any Jamaican musician and they'll tell you the rocksteady days were the best days of Jamaican music."[[14]](#cite_note-Music-14)

## Transformation into reggae

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rocksteady&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Transformation into reggae")]

Several factors contributed to the evolution of rocksteady into reggae in the late 1960s. The emigration to [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") of key musical [arrangers](/wiki/Arrangement "Arrangement") [Jackie Mittoo](/wiki/Jackie_Mittoo "Jackie Mittoo") and [Lynn Taitt](/wiki/Lynn_Taitt "Lynn Taitt")—and the upgrading of Jamaican studio technology—had a marked effect on the sound and style of the recordings. Bass patterns became more complex and increasingly dominated the arrangements, and the [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano") gave way to the [electric organ](/wiki/Electronic_organ "Electronic organ"). Other developments included horns fading farther into the background; the introduction of a scratchier, more percussive rhythm guitar; the addition of African-style hand drumming, and a more precise, intricate and aggressive drumming style.

Also around this time (1969–70) the use of a vocal-free or lead instrument-free dub or B-side "version" became popular in Jamaica; at the beginning this involved the use of rocksteady tracks, most notably with U-Roy [deejaying](/wiki/Deejay_(Jamaican) "Deejay (Jamaican)") over Treasure Isle rhythms (made by a young Osbourne Ruddock, later known as [King Tubby](/wiki/King_Tubby "King Tubby"), beginning with "Wake the Town"). Indeed, this collaboration provided rocksteady with an afterlife as U-Roy rocksteady-based songs rode high in the charts (1970–71), even as reggae began to establish itself as the new sound.

By the late 1960s, the [Rastafari movement](/wiki/Rastafari_movement "Rastafari movement") became more popular in Jamaica and rocksteady became less popular.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Many reggae songs became focused less on romance and more on black consciousness, politics and protest. The release of the 1972 film *[The Harder They Come](/wiki/The_Harder_They_Come "The Harder They Come")* and the rise of Jamaican superstar Bob Marley brought reggae to an international level that rocksteady never reached.

Although rocksteady was a short-lived phase of Jamaican popular music, its influence on what came after: reggae, [dub](/wiki/Dub_music "Dub music") and [dancehall](/wiki/Dancehall "Dancehall") is significant. Many bass lines originally created for rocksteady songs continue to be used in contemporary Jamaican music. Such as the rhythm from "Never Let Go" by [Slim Smith](/wiki/Slim_Smith "Slim Smith") (sometimes known as the 'answer rhythm') and "Real Rock" both from the Studio One label; "My Conversation" also sung by Slim Smith, produced by Bunny Lee; "Queen Majesty" sung by the Techniques and "Lonely Street" by the Conquerors, both for Treasure Isle label.
